::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="How to read this report"}

A short primer on the statistical vocabulary these reports assume, written for a reader who knows frequentist statistics but is newer to Bayesian methods. The project glossary in `METHODS.md` covers each idea in more depth.

- **Posterior distribution.** The model's answer is not one number but a distribution over the plausible values of each quantity, combining the prior (what was assumed before seeing the data) with the data. Every estimate below is a summary of a posterior — usually its median plus an interval.
- **Credible interval.** An 89% credible interval is a range that holds the quantity with 89% probability, given the model and data — the direct probability statement people often wrongly attach to a frequentist confidence interval (which instead describes how the interval-building procedure performs over repeated samples). Each quantity is reported as its posterior median with an inner 50% and outer 89% equal-tailed interval, alongside the full posterior. The 89% coverage is deliberately non-round: 95% is an arbitrary NHST convention, and its tail limits are the least MCMC-stable part of the posterior.
- **Credible vs prediction interval.** A credible interval describes uncertainty about an average quantity such as the treatment effect; a prediction interval describes where an individual child's score might fall, which is always much wider. The intervals in the findings box are credible intervals.
- **The ROPE and δ.** The ROPE (region of practical equivalence) is a band around zero, from −δ to +δ, inside which an effect is judged too small to matter in practice. In this reanalysis the education lead agreed the minimally important difference δ after the first results had been examined, so it is a transparent post-hoc judgement rather than a prospectively specified threshold. Every ITT report therefore shows how the conclusion changes across alternative δ values instead of resting on one cut-off.
- **Causal vs association labels.** Only effects backed by randomisation are labelled *causal*. Random assignment makes treatment allocation independent of baseline characteristics and balances groups on average across repeated randomisations; it does not guarantee exact balance in this finite sample. Every other coefficient is an *adjusted association* — it may reflect confounding, so it is never read as "X drives Y", and the reports enforce that labelling rather than leaving it to the reader.
- **The logit scale and items.** The models work on the logit (log-odds) scale, which handles bounded test scores cleanly, but logits are hard to read — so headline effects are translated back into test items (or percentage points for the floored measures).
- **Beta-Binomial.** The likelihood treats each test score as a number of successes out of the test's maximum, with the Beta part absorbing child-to-child overdispersion.
- **The convergence gate.** Before any estimate is interpreted, the sampler's output must pass automatic checks (R-hat, effective sample size, divergences, sampling energy); if it fails, the report withholds the findings box and flags the fit for review.

:::
